was a [parliamentary constituency](/wiki/United_Kingdom_constituencies "United Kingdom constituencies"), in existence between 1918 and 1950, of the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"). It elected one [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) by the [first past the post](/wiki/First_past_the_post "First past the post") system of election.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Parliamentary constituency in the United Kingdom, 1918–1950

| Seaham | |
| --- | --- |
| Former [county constituency](/wiki/County_constituency "County constituency") for the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") | |
| [1918](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election")–[1950](/wiki/1950_United_Kingdom_general_election "1950 United Kingdom general election") | |
| Seats | one |
| Created from | [South East Durham](/wiki/South_East_Durham "South East Durham") and part of [Houghton-le-Spring](/wiki/Houghton-le-Spring_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Houghton-le-Spring (UK Parliament constituency)") |
| Replaced by | [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Houghton-le-Spring](/wiki/Houghton-le-Spring_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Houghton-le-Spring (UK Parliament constituency)") |

**Seaham** was a [parliamentary constituency](/wiki/United_Kingdom_constituencies "United Kingdom constituencies"), in existence between 1918 and 1950, of the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"). It elected one [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) by the [first past the post](/wiki/First_past_the_post "First past the post") system of election.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seaham_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

**Seaham** was created under the [Representation of the People Act 1918](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1918 "Representation of the People Act 1918") for the [1918 general election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election"), comprising northern parts of the abolished [South Eastern Division of Durham](/wiki/South_East_Durham "South East Durham"). The town of [Seaham](/wiki/Seaham "Seaham") itself was transferred from [Houghton-le-Spring](/wiki/Houghton-le-Spring_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Houghton-le-Spring (UK Parliament constituency)").

It was abolished for the [1950 general election](/wiki/1950_United_Kingdom_general_election "1950 United Kingdom general election") under the [Representation of the People Act 1948](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948 "Representation of the People Act 1948"), with the bulk of its area moved into the new constituency of [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)"). The expanded Urban District of Seaham Harbour (now incorporating Seaham) was transferred back to Houghton-le-Spring.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Boundaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seaham_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Boundaries")]

* The Urban District of Seaham Harbour; and
* the Rural District of Easington.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Political history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seaham_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Political history")]

The history of this constituency, which incorporated a lot of the mining area of the eastern part of [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") around [Seaham](/wiki/Seaham "Seaham"), is of strong [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") support.

In the so-called "[coupon election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election")" of 1918, Major [Evan Hayward](/wiki/Evan_Hayward "Evan Hayward") was issued a [Coalition 'coupon'](/wiki/The_Coalition_Coupon "The Coalition Coupon"). However, he repudiated the 'coupon' and stood as a [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") and was elected. At the following [general election, in 1922](/wiki/1922_United_Kingdom_general_election "1922 United Kingdom general election"), [Sidney Webb](/wiki/Sidney_Webb "Sidney Webb"), an early socialist and author of the Labour Party's then-new constitution, was returned. Webb was easily re-elected in [1923](/wiki/1923_United_Kingdom_general_election "1923 United Kingdom general election") and [1924](/wiki/1924_United_Kingdom_general_election "1924 United Kingdom general election"). He was subsequently raised to the peerage; his successor as parliamentary candidate was [Ramsay MacDonald](/wiki/Ramsay_MacDonald "Ramsay MacDonald"), the leader of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") at the time. At the [1929 general election](/wiki/1929_United_Kingdom_general_election "1929 United Kingdom general election") MacDonald won, and for the second time became Prime Minister presiding over a minority Labour administration.

The economic crisis after 1929 led to a political crisis in mid-1931, and MacDonald failed to secure agreement in cabinet for his proposed cuts in [outdoor relief](/wiki/Outdoor_relief "Outdoor relief") for the unemployed. MacDonald went to see King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V"), who persuaded him to form a [National Government](/wiki/First_National_ministry "First National ministry"). In the general election that followed, MacDonald stood in Seaham as [National Labour](/wiki/National_Labour_Organisation "National Labour Organisation") and was comfortably elected, and continued to serve as a Prime Minister of a National Government that was predominantly Conservative-supported.

MacDonald retired as prime minister in 1935 but remained in the Cabinet. In the [general election of 1935](/wiki/1935_United_Kingdom_general_election "1935 United Kingdom general election") he was resoundingly defeated at Seaham by [Emanuel Shinwell](/wiki/Emanuel_Shinwell "Emanuel Shinwell"), the Labour Party candidate. Shinwell was re-elected in the Labour landslide at the [1945 election](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election") and subsequently served as MP for the successor constituency of [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)") until 1970.

**General Election 1939–40**

Another General Election was required to take place before the end of 1940. The political parties had been making preparations for an election to take place and by the Autumn of 1939, the following candidates had been selected;

* **Labour**: [Manny Shinwell](/wiki/Manny_Shinwell,_Baron_Shinwell "Manny Shinwell, Baron Shinwell")[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* **Conservative**:
